14.anchor: For the entire twelve years that I was governor of Judah—from the twentieth year to the thirty-second year of the reign of King Artaxerxes

15.anchor: The former governors, in contrast, had laid heavy burdens on the people, demanding a daily ration of food and wine, besides forty pieces

1.anchor: Early the following spring, in the month of Nisan,
1.note: Hebrew In the month of Nisan.

7.anchor: I also said to the king, “If it please the king, let me have letters addressed to the governors of the province west of the Euphrates River,
7.note: Hebrew the province beyond the river;

9.anchor: When I came to the governors of the province west of the Euphrates River, I delivered the king’s letters to them. The king, I should add, had sent along army officers and horsemen

13.anchor: After dark I went out through the Valley Gate, past the Jackal’s Well,

15.anchor: So, though it was still dark, I went up the Kidron Valley

2.anchor: saying in front of his friends and the Samarian army officers, “What does this bunch of poor, feeble Jews think they’re doing? Do they think they can build the wall in a single day by just offering a few sacrifices?
2.note: The meaning of the Hebrew is uncertain.

5.anchor: Do not ignore their guilt. Do not blot out their sins, for they have provoked you to anger here in front of
5.note: Or for they have thrown insults in the face of.

12.anchor: The Jews who lived near the enemy came and told us again and again, “They will come from all directions and attack us!”
12.note: The meaning of the Hebrew is uncertain.

23.anchor: During this time, none of us—not I, nor my relatives, nor my servants, nor the guards who were with me—ever took off our clothes. We carried our weapons with us at all times, even when we went for water.
23.note: Or Each carried his weapon in his right hand. Each his weapon the water.

3.anchor: I said to them, “Do not leave the gates open during the hottest part of the day.
3.note: Or Keep the gates of Jerusalem closed until the sun is hot.

7.anchor: Their leaders were Zerubbabel, Jeshua, Nehemiah, Seraiah,
7.note: As in parallel text at Ezra 2:2; Hebrew reads Azariah.
As in parallel text at Ezra 2:2; Hebrew reads Raamiah.
As in parallel text at Ezra 2:2; Hebrew reads Mispereth.
As in parallel text at Ezra 2:2; Hebrew reads Nehum.

15.note: As in parallel text at Ezra 2:10; Hebrew reads Binnui.

24.note: As in parallel text at Ezra 2:18; Hebrew reads Hariph.

25.note: As in parallel text at Ezra 2:20; Hebrew reads Gibeon.

43.anchor: The families of Jeshua and Kadmiel (descendants of Hodaviah
43.note: As in parallel text at Ezra 2:40; Hebrew reads Hodevah.

47.note: As in parallel text at Ezra 2:44; Hebrew reads Sia.

52.note: As in parallel text at Ezra 2:50; Hebrew reads Nephushesim.

54.note: As in parallel text at Ezra 2:52; Hebrew reads Bazlith.

57.note: As in parallel text at Ezra 2:55; Hebrew reads Sotai, Sophereth, Perida.

58.note: As in parallel text at Ezra 2:56; Hebrew reads Jaala.

59.note: As in parallel text at Ezra 2:57; Hebrew reads Amon.

61.anchor: Another group returned at this time from the towns of Tel-melah, Tel-harsha, Kerub, Addan,
61.note: As in parallel text at Ezra 2:59; Hebrew reads Addon.

68.anchor: They took with them 736 horses, 245 mules,
68.note: As in some Hebrew manuscripts (see also Ezra 2:66); most Hebrew manuscripts lack this verse. Verses 7:69-73 are numbered 7:68-72 in Hebrew text.

70.anchor: Some of the family leaders gave gifts for the work. The governor gave to the treasury 1,000 gold coins,

71.anchor: The other leaders gave to the treasury a total of 20,000 gold coins

72.anchor: The rest of the people gave 20,000 gold coins, about 2,500 pounds

2.anchor: So Sanballat and Geshem sent a message asking me to meet them at one of the villages
2.note: As in Greek version; Hebrew reads at Kephirim.

6.anchor: “There is a rumor among the surrounding nations, and Geshem

9.anchor: They were just trying to intimidate us, imagining that they could discourage us and stop the work. So I continued the work with even greater determination.
9.note: As in Greek version; Hebrew reads But now to strengthen my hands.

15.note: Hebrew on the twenty-fifth day of the month Elul,

2.note: Hebrew on the first day of the seventh month,

14.anchor: had commanded through Moses that the Israelites should live in shelters during the festival to be held that month.

15.anchor: He had said that a proclamation should be made throughout their towns and in Jerusalem, telling the people to go to the hills to get branches from olive, wild olive,

17.anchor: So everyone who had returned from captivity lived in these shelters during the festival, and they were all filled with great joy! The Israelites had not celebrated like this since the days of Joshua

1.anchor: In late autumn, in the month of Kislev, in the twentieth year of King Artaxerxes’ reign,
1.note: Hebrew In the month of Kislev of the twentieth year. twentieth year

9.anchor: But if you return to me and obey my commands and live by them, then even if you are exiled to the ends of the earth,

11.anchor: O Lord, please hear my prayer! Listen to the prayers of those of us who delight in honoring you. Please grant me success today by making the king favorable to me.
11.note: Hebrew today in the sight of this man.

5.anchor: Also Maaseiah son of Baruch, son of Col-hozeh, son of Hazaiah, son of Adaiah, son of Joiarib, son of Zechariah, of the family of Shelah.

14.note: As in Greek version; Hebrew reads their.

35.anchor: Lod, Ono, and the Valley of Craftsmen.

1.note: Hebrew On the twenty-fourth day of that same month,

3.anchor: They remained standing in place for three hours
3.note: Hebrew for a quarter of a day.

9.anchor: “You saw the misery of our ancestors in Egypt, and you heard their cries from beside the Red Sea.

17.anchor: They refused to obey and did not remember the miracles you had done for them. Instead, they became stubborn and appointed a leader to take them back to their slavery in Egypt.
17.note: As in Greek version; Hebrew reads in their rebellion.

22.anchor: “Then you helped our ancestors conquer kingdoms and nations, and you placed your people in every corner of the land.
22.note: The meaning of the Hebrew is uncertain.

38.anchor: The people responded, “In view of all this,
38.note: Or In spite of all this.

32.anchor: “In addition, we promise to obey the command to pay the annual Temple tax of one-eighth of an ounce of silver
32.note: Hebrew tax of 1/3 of a shekel

6.note: Or The Mishneh Gate, The Jeshanah Gate.

7.anchor: Next to them were Melatiah from Gibeon, Jadon from Meronoth, people from Gibeon, and people from Mizpah, the headquarters of the governor of the province west of the Euphrates River.
7.note: Hebrew the province beyond the river.

8.anchor: Next was Uzziel son of Harhaiah, a goldsmith by trade, who also worked on the wall. Beyond him was Hananiah, a manufacturer of perfumes. They left out a section of Jerusalem as they built the Broad Wall.
8.note: Or They fortified Jerusalem up to the Broad Wall.

13.anchor: The Valley Gate was repaired by the people from Zanoah, led by Hanun. They set up its doors and installed its bolts and bars. They also repaired the 1,500 feet

15.anchor: The Fountain Gate was repaired by Shallum
15.note: As in Syriac version; Hebrew reads Shallun.

15.anchor: son of Col-hozeh, the leader of the Mizpah district. He rebuilt it, roofed it, set up its doors, and installed its bolts and bars. Then he repaired the wall of the pool of Siloam

18.anchor: Next down the line were his countrymen led by Binnui
18.note: As in a few Hebrew manuscripts, some Greek manuscripts, and Syriac version (see also 3:24; 10:9); most Hebrew manuscripts read Bavvai.

1.anchor: On that same day, as the Book of Moses was being read to the people, the passage was found that said no Ammonite or Moabite should ever be permitted to enter the assembly of God.

6.anchor: I was not in Jerusalem at that time, for I had returned to King Artaxerxes of Babylon in the thirty-second year of his reign,
6.note: King Artaxerxes of Persia is here identified as the king of Babylon because Persia had conquered the Babylonian Empire. The thirty-second year of Artaxerxes was 433 b.c.

19.anchor: Then I commanded that the gates of Jerusalem should be shut as darkness fell every Friday evening,
19.note: Hebrew on the day before the Sabbath.

4.note: As in some Hebrew manuscripts and Latin Vulgate (see also 12:16); most Hebrew manuscripts read Ginnethoi.

11.anchor: Joiada was the father of Johanan.

14.anchor: Jonathan was leader of the family of Malluch.
14.note: As in Greek version (see also 10:4; 12:2); Hebrew reads Malluchi.

14.anchor: Joseph was leader of the family of Shecaniah.
14.note: As in many Hebrew manuscripts, some Greek manuscripts, and Syriac version (see also 12:3); most Hebrew manuscripts read Shebaniah.

15.anchor: Helkai was leader of the family of Meremoth.
15.note: As in some Greek manuscripts (see also 12:3); Hebrew reads Meraioth.

17.note: Hebrew lacks the name of this family leader.

20.anchor: Kallai was leader of the family of Sallu.

22.anchor: A record of the Levite families was kept during the years when Eliashib, Joiada, Johanan, and Jaddua served as high priest. Another record of the priests was kept during the reign of Darius the Persian.
22.note: is probably Darius II, who reigned 423–404 b.c. Darius the Persian

23.anchor: down to the days of Johanan, the grandson

24.anchor: These were the family leaders of the Levites: Hashabiah, Sherebiah, Jeshua, Binnui,

26.anchor: These all served in the days of Joiakim son of Jeshua, son of Jehozadak,

31.anchor: I led the leaders of Judah to the top of the wall and organized two large choirs to give thanks. One of the choirs proceeded southward

38.anchor: The second choir giving thanks went northward

39.anchor: then past the Ephraim Gate to the Old City Gate,
39.note: Or the Mishneh Gate, the Jeshanah Gate.
